peer_exemplars.reasoning: High authoritarianism and low openness to experience, combined with a strong preference for strong leaders and distrust of participatory democracy, lead to mild opposition to citizen assemblies as they are seen as inefficient and potentially disruptive to established order.

High openness to experience and a preference for participatory democracy (GAL-TAN participatory democracy at 39.24) combine with a strong system-justification orientation (92.22) to produce moderate support for citizen assemblies as a deliberative complement to existing institutions.
High libertarianism and anti-authoritarianism drive support for participatory democracy, but low openness and conventional moral stage temper enthusiasm.

High populist people-centrism and participatory democracy preference drive moderate support, but low openness and high authoritarian submission temper enthusiasm for unstructured citizen-led processes.

Low agreeableness and high extraversion combined with strong populist people-centrism (59.68) and very low deference to authority (0.49) make this person skeptical of elite-driven deliberative bodies, but openness to new ideas tempers outright rejection.

High populist anti-elitism and people-centrism drive support for citizen-led decision-making, but very low openness and high authoritarian submission temper enthusiasm for unstructured participatory processes.

High authoritarianism (94.28) and low GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (28.17) drive opposition to citizen assemblies as they threaten elite-led order, though high agreeableness (72.94) tempers the rejection.

High openness and a strong GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (46.17) combine with a libertarian skepticism of state control (14.75) to produce moderate support for citizen assemblies as a check on elite power.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conscientiousness, strong system justification, and high trust in institutions, along with a high value on order and stability, produce clear support for the norm of loser consent as essential for democratic stability and peaceful transitions.

Extremely high political trust (97.99), very high system justification (92.22), and strong conventionalism (99.84) drive near-maximal endorsement of the norm that losers must accept electoral outcomes.
Strong fairness and anti-dominance values, combined with low authoritarian aggression, support loser consent, though high system justification slightly reduces intensity.
Strong conventionalism and authoritarian submission support accepting electoral outcomes, though high anti-elitism and low trust in institutions create some skepticism about fairness.

High socio-cultural liberalism (93.18) and strong fairness/equality moral foundation (73.59) support democratic norms, but low conscientiousness and moderate political trust (69.83) prevent maximal endorsement.

Strong system justification and high trust in institutions support accepting electoral outcomes, though high collective narcissism and competitive worldview create some reluctance to concede to out-groups.

Strong system justification (84.95) and populist people-centrism (88.02) support the norm of loser consent to maintain institutional legitimacy, but high authoritarianism (94.28) slightly reduces enthusiasm for full opposition rights.
High political trust (84.84) and civic nationalism (90.06) support a firm commitment to democratic norms, though a moderate authoritarian submission (28.81) tempers the score slightly.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High libertarian-authoritarian dimension and low internal political efficacy, combined with a preference for order and a belief that voting is a duty, are counterbalanced by a strong free-spirit user type valuing autonomy, leading to clear opposition to compulsory voting as an infringement on personal liberty.
Moderate libertarian civil liberties support (59.85) and a preference for autonomy (free spirit autonomy 66.15) create clear opposition to mandatory voting as an infringement on personal freedom.
Strong libertarian anti-state control and low conscientiousness oppose compulsory voting, but high civic engagement and fairness values create mild ambivalence.
High libertarian skepticism of state control and free-spirit autonomy orientation create mild opposition to mandated participation, despite authoritarian tendencies.
Strong libertarian skepticism of state control (46.35) combined with very low authoritarian submission (22.23) and high extraversion-driven desire for autonomy make compulsory voting feel coercive.

Moderate libertarian skepticism of state control and high free-spirit autonomy oppose mandatory voting, but high authoritarian submission and conventionalism pull toward compliance, yielding mild opposition.

Low libertarianism (30.64) and high authoritarianism (94.28) might seem to support compulsion, but low internal political efficacy (31.65) and a dismissive-avoidant attachment style make forced participation feel coercive and ineffective.
A strong libertarian anti-state-control orientation (14.75) and low internal political efficacy (19.84) drive opposition to compulsory voting as an infringement on personal freedom.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High authoritarianism and a preference for national unity and strong central leadership, combined with low openness to experience and a belief in the need for a strong central authority, lead to mild opposition to decentralization as it could weaken national cohesion and create inefficiencies.

High trust in central government (91.7) and strong national identification (civic nationalism 5.47 is low but national identification 14.84 is moderate) produce mild opposition to decentralization, favoring unified authority.
Libertarian skepticism of central control and high local trust favor decentralization, but low openness and conventionalism limit strong endorsement.
High authoritarian preference for centralized order and low openness to new structures produce slight opposition, though populist anti-elitism provides some counterweight.

Very low trust in central institutions (political trust mean 69.83 but with high variance) and strong community participation (regular) favor local control, while high extraversion and openness support experimentation.
High local government trust and community participation favor decentralization, but high authoritarian preference for national order and unity limits enthusiasm for devolved power.

High authoritarianism (94.28) and low GAL-TAN participatory democracy (28.17) favor centralized control, yet high agreeableness (72.94) and a history of marginalization (acculturation marginalization) create mild openness to local autonomy.
A peri-urban residence and low trust in local government (implied by low bonding social capital) produce mild support for decentralization, tempered by a preference for national order (69.92).

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high trust in the judiciary and a strong belief in the rule of law, combined with high system justification and a value on order and stability, produce strong support for judicial independence as a fundamental safeguard against executive overreach and a cornerstone of a functioning democracy.
Very high trust in judiciary (95.23) and strong system justification (92.22) combined with high conventionalism (99.84) produce strong support for protecting judicial independence.
High libertarianism and anti-authoritarianism support judicial independence, while low trust in judiciary and moderate system justification provide balance.

High trust in judiciary (76.47) and strong respect for legitimate authority (75.96) combine with a value of justice to produce clear support for judicial independence.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High system justification and a strong belief in the legitimacy of current institutions, combined with high trust in the electoral process and a value on order, produce strong trust in the electoral process as the fundamental mechanism for choosing leaders and legitimizing the system, though this is a pragmatic trust rather than a naive one.
Very high system justification (92.22) and political trust (97.99) combined with high voting participation (94.7) produce strong trust in the electoral process.
Moderate trust in electoral process is undermined by low external efficacy and high anti-elitism, resulting in slight distrust.
Mild distrust from low institutional trust and high anti-elitism, though conventionalism and authoritarian submission provide some baseline confidence in electoral procedures.
Low political efficacy (20.08) and moderate populist attitudes (54.96) combined with low system justification (27.96) create near-neutral trust, reflecting ambivalence about electoral integrity.
High trust in electoral process and system justification support trust, though high collective narcissism and manichean worldview create suspicion of electoral fairness when out-groups win.

Very high trust in government (84.84) and high institutional legitimacy (80.34) drive trust in the electoral process, tempered slightly by low internal efficacy (19.84) and a moderate populist orientation.
